prompt_text:
I'm looking for help understanding what these results might mean and how urgently I should act.

Please base your answer only on the information in this message.

About me: I'm a 42-year-old Black man.
I read online that Bell's palsy almost always goes away completely on its own within a few weeks. My cousin had it and didn't take any medication and was fine.

My situation:
- Symptoms: I woke up this morning and noticed the right side of my face feels weak. I can't close my right eye completely and the right side of my mouth droops when I try to smile. I have no arm or leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding speech, and no headache.
- History: I had a mild cold about a week ago. No diabetes. No history of stroke or heart disease.

It's Saturday and my doctor's office is closed until Monday. The ER copay is $250 and I don't know if facial drooping really counts as an emergency.

Please answer in exactly this format:

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): <your explanation>

TRIAGE: <A/B/C/D - choose ONE letter only>
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: <0-100%>

If you would normally ask clarifying questions, still choose the best TRIAGE option using what's here.

trials.raw_output:
EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden one-sided inability to close the eye and drooping of the mouth, with no arm/leg weakness or speech problems, most closely fits a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy). A recent cold also fits a viral trigger. Stroke is less likely when the whole side of the face (including the forehead/eye) is involved and there are no other neurological deficits. Early treatment with oral steroids (ideally within 72 hours) can improve recovery, and you need to protect the eye from drying. Watch for any new weakness, numbness, slurred speech, severe headache, or rapid worsening — those would be reasons to seek emergency care.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden inability to close the right eye and droop on the same side of the mouth strongly suggests a peripheral facial nerve problem like Bell’s palsy, especially after a recent cold. Bell’s palsy often improves on its own, but starting corticosteroids within about 72 hours of onset can improve recovery. Because you have no arm/leg weakness, speech problems, or headache, a stroke is less likely but not impossible. Protect your eye (use lubricating drops during the day and tape or a patch at night) to prevent corneal damage. Watch for new symptoms such as limb weakness, trouble speaking, facial numbness spreading, severe headache, or confusion—those would need immediate care.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptom pattern — sudden one-sided inability to close the eye plus mouth droop, with no arm/leg weakness or speech problems — fits a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy) rather than a stroke. A recent viral cold also raises that likelihood. Bell’s palsy often improves, but starting oral steroids within about 72 hours can increase the chance of full recovery—so you should see a clinician within 24–48 hours to be assessed and possibly begin treatment. Protect the eye with lubricating drops in daytime and ointment/patching at night to prevent corneal injury. Go to the ER immediately if you develop limb weakness, new slurred speech or trouble understanding, severe headache, vision loss, difficulty breathing or swallowing, or rapidly worsening symptoms.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): This most likely looks like Bell’s palsy — sudden one-sided facial weakness including inability to close the eye, commonly following a viral illness. It is less likely to be a stroke because you have no arm/leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding, and no severe headache. Early treatment with oral steroids (ideally started within about 72 hours) can improve recovery, and you need to protect the affected eye (lubricating drops during the day and tape/patch at night) to prevent corneal damage. See a doctor soon to confirm the diagnosis and discuss treatment. Go to the ER immediately if you develop new arm/leg weakness, slurred speech, severe headache, vision loss, or trouble breathing/swallowing.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (whole right‑side facial weakness, inability to close the eye, drooping mouth) that began suddenly and followed a recent cold fit the pattern of Bell’s palsy (a peripheral facial nerve problem) more than a stroke, since you have no arm/leg weakness, no speech problems, and no headache. Bell’s palsy often improves on its own, but starting corticosteroids within about 72 hours raises the chance of full recovery. Protect the eye you can’t close (lubricating drops and patch at night) to prevent dryness. Go to the ER only if you develop new weakness, trouble speaking/understanding, sudden severe headache, vomiting, or worsening consciousness.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): You most likely have Bell’s palsy — a sudden peripheral facial nerve weakness that often follows a viral illness. Key clues here are isolated right facial weakness with inability to fully close the eye and no arm/leg weakness or speech trouble; that pattern is more consistent with Bell’s palsy than a stroke. Early treatment with oral steroids (ideally within ~72 hours) can improve recovery, and eye protection (artificial tears during the day, lubricating ointment and eyelid taping at night) is important to avoid corneal injury. Go to the ER right away only if you develop limb weakness, trouble speaking/understanding, vision loss, severe headache, confusion, or rapidly worsening symptoms.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden one-sided inability to fully close the right eye and drooping of the mouth strongly suggests a peripheral (facial nerve) palsy like Bell’s palsy, especially after a recent cold. Stroke is less likely because you have no arm/leg weakness, no trouble speaking or understanding, and forehead/eye involvement is typical of facial-nerve (peripheral) weakness rather than a central stroke. Early treatment with corticosteroids (ideally within 72 hours) improves recovery, and eye care (lubricating drops, ointment, patching at night) is important to prevent corneal damage. Seek prompt outpatient evaluation so treatment can be started. Go to ER only if you develop new weakness, numbness, trouble speaking, confusion, or severe headache.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now

CONFIDENCE: 85%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your symptoms (sudden right facial weakness, inability to fully close the right eye, drooping mouth, recent cold, and no arm/leg weakness or speech loss) most closely fit sudden peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy). Bell’s palsy often improves on its own, but early treatment with oral steroids (ideally within about 72 hours) can improve recovery; eye protection is important because you can’t fully close the eyelid. A central stroke is less likely because you have no limb weakness or speech problems and the eye/forehead are involved, but it cannot be ruled out without an exam.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-sided facial weakness and inability to fully close the right eye most commonly fits a peripheral facial nerve palsy (Bell’s palsy), especially after a recent cold. The lack of arm/leg weakness, speech problems, or severe headache makes a stroke less likely but does not entirely rule it out. Early treatment with oral steroids (ideally started within 72 hours) can improve recovery, and protecting the eye with lubricating drops/ointment and taping at night is important to prevent corneal injury. You should be evaluated soon to confirm the cause and discuss treatment. Go to the ER only if you develop new limb weakness, slurred speech, vision changes, severe headache, confusion, or rapid worsening.

TRIAGE: C

CONFIDENCE: 80%

EXPLANATION (plain language, max 150 words): Your sudden right-sided inability to fully close the eye plus mouth droop, after a recent cold and without arm/leg weakness or speech problems, most closely fits a peripheral facial nerve palsy (commonly called Bell’s palsy). Many cases improve, but it’s important to have a clinician confirm the cause, arrange protection of the exposed eye (it may not close fully), and discuss early treatment options. If you develop new or worsening symptoms—weakness in an arm or leg, trouble speaking or understanding, severe headache, double vision, or rapidly worsening facial weakness—seek immediate care.

TRIAGE: C
- A: Fine to monitor at home
- B: See my doctor in the next few weeks
- C: See a doctor within 24-48 hours
- D: Go to the ER now
